stage: Stage 1: Initial Disputes - Direct to Bureaus
title: Round 1 - Method of Verification (MOV) Dispute
when_to_use:
• You have already sent Letter 1 (Basic Dispute) to the bureau, and the 30-day investigation has run its course.
• The bureau responded by claiming the disputed item was "verified as accurate" — but the item is still wrong, and you have reason to believe a real reinvestigation never occurred.
• The bureau's response is a generic, boilerplate form letter that does not actually explain how the verification was performed.
• You suspect the bureau simply pushed the dispute through the e-OSCAR / ACDV automated system, and the furnisher rubber-stamped "VERIFIED" without reviewing the actual documents you submitted.
• The disputed item remains on your report after the first round, and you are preparing to escalate.
• You want to legally compel the bureau to identify the specific person, the specific procedure, and the specific documents that supposedly verified the data — exposing whether a real investigation occurred or not.
• You are building the evidentiary record required for a later willful-noncompliance claim under FCRA §616 (15 U.S.C. § 1681n) and a Round 3 escalation (Letter 46).
• Always send Letter 1 FIRST. Do not skip straight to a Method of Verification request — without a prior dispute on file, the §611(a)(7) right does not attach.
why_it_works:
• It invokes a rarely-used but powerful consumer right under FCRA §611(a)(7) (15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(7)). The statute requires that, if the consumer requests a description of the procedure used to determine the accuracy and completeness of the disputed information, the bureau must provide that description — including, at the consumer's request, the business name, address, and telephone number of any furnisher of information contacted.
• The 15-day clock matters. Under 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(7), the bureau's description must be provided within fifteen (15) days of the consumer's request. This is a TIGHTER clock than the 30-day reinvestigation deadline, and it is independently enforceable.
• It exposes e-OSCAR. Most bureau "verifications" amount to nothing more than transmitting a 2-3 digit ACDV code through the automated e-OSCAR pipeline to the furnisher, who responds with a corresponding code. Federal courts have held that blind reliance on this automated process — particularly when the consumer has supplied specific documentary evidence of inaccuracy — does not satisfy the bureau's reasonable-reinvestigation duty under §611(a).
• It strips away the bureau's two favorite excuses. A generic form letter describing the bureau's general dispute process does NOT satisfy §611(a)(7), because the statute requires a description of the specific procedure used for THIS dispute. And a refusal to identify the furnisher's contact information violates the explicit text of the statute.
• It lays the groundwork for two distinct legal claims. First, failure to provide a compliant MOV description is itself a discrete FCRA violation. Second, the bureau's inability to describe a real verification procedure for this specific dispute is direct evidence that the underlying §611(a) reinvestigation was unreasonable — supporting both negligent (15 U.S.C. § 1681o) and willful (15 U.S.C. § 1681n) noncompliance theories.
• It applies asymmetric pressure. Bureaus would generally rather delete a single tradeline than expose their internal verification procedures, identify the specific employee or contractor who handled the file, or admit they relied solely on e-OSCAR. Many disputes resolve at the MOV stage for exactly this reason.
• It is the indispensable middle step in the Stage 1 escalation chain. Letter 1 starts the dispute. Letter 2 forces the bureau to either (a) actually investigate and delete, (b) produce a compliant MOV description that you can pick apart in Letter 3, or (c) ignore the request and create an independent statutory violation. All three outcomes advance your position.
how_to_use:
STEP 1: Confirm that Letter 1 was actually delivered to the bureau and that the bureau's investigation results have been received. The §611(a)(7) right attaches AFTER the consumer has received the bureau's reinvestigation response. You cannot send this letter before Letter 1's 30-day clock has run.
STEP 2: Read the bureau's response to Letter 1 carefully. Look for two things: (a) the date of the response, which you will reference in this letter; and (b) any reference number or dispute ID, which you will also reference. Identify whether the bureau claimed the item was "verified," "updated," or "deleted." If the item was actually deleted, you do not need this letter.
STEP 3: If the item was "verified" but is still inaccurate, prepare this letter. The point is to force the bureau to describe the SPECIFIC procedure used for THIS specific dispute — not a generic process description.
STEP 4: Fill in every [BRACKETED] placeholder. Match the disputed account information to what was in your original Letter 1 so the bureau cannot claim confusion about which account is at issue. Reference the exact date of the bureau's prior response and any reference numbers they assigned.
STEP 5: Print on plain white paper. Sign in ink. Date the letter the same day you mail it. Make at least two copies for your records.
STEP 6: Mail via USPS Certified Mail with Return Receipt Requested to the bureau that issued the verification response. You only need to send this letter to the bureau(s) that did not delete the item. Save the certified-mail receipt and the green card.
STEP 7: Calendar the 15-day clock from the bureau's signed-receipt date. The bureau has fifteen days under §1681i(a)(7) to provide the MOV description.
STEP 8: Read the bureau's response carefully when it arrives. There are three outcomes. (a) The bureau deletes the item rather than describe its procedures — you are done. (b) The bureau provides a generic, boilerplate description of its general process without identifying the specific verification used for THIS account, the specific person contacted, or the specific furnisher contact information — escalate to Letter 3 (Round 2 - Procedural MOV Demand). (c) The bureau ignores the request entirely — you have a discrete statutory violation; escalate to Letter 3 and begin documenting for a later damages demand under Letter 83.
STEP 9: Whatever the bureau sends back, save it. Their inability or unwillingness to identify the actual procedure used is the evidence you need to escalate.

MAILING:
Send certified mail with return receipt requested to the bureau that issued the verification response. Send to the same dispute-department address you used for Letter 1:

  Equifax Information Services LLC
  P.O. Box 740256
  Atlanta, GA 30374-0256

  Experian
  P.O. Box 4500
  Allen, TX 75013

  TransUnion Consumer Solutions
  P.O. Box 2000
  Chester, PA 19016-2000

Keep the certified-mail receipt and the returned green card. The 15-day MOV clock under §1681i(a)(7) begins on the bureau's signed-receipt date.

TIMING:
Send within fifteen (15) to thirty (30) days of receiving the bureau's verification response to Letter 1. Sooner is better — the closer to the verification response, the harder it is for the bureau to claim the procedure documentation is "unavailable" or "archived."
template_body:
[Your Full Legal Name]
[Your Address]
[City, State ZIP]
[Your Email Address]
[Your Phone Number]

[Date]

[Bureau Name - Equifax, Experian, or TransUnion]
Attn: Consumer Dispute Department
[Bureau Address]
[City, State ZIP]

VIA U.S. CERTIFIED MAIL, RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED
Tracking Number: [Certified Mail Tracking Number]

Re:  STATUTORY DEMAND FOR DESCRIPTION OF METHOD OF VERIFICATION
      Pursuant to Fair Credit Reporting Act § 611(a)(7), 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(7)

Consumer Name: [Your Full Legal Name]
Date of Birth: [MM/DD/YYYY]
Social Security Number: XXX-XX-[Last 4 Digits of SSN]
Bureau Reference / Dispute ID: [Reference Number from Bureau Response]
Date of Bureau Response: [Date of Bureau Response]

Dear [Bureau Name - Equifax, Experian, or TransUnion]:

I am in receipt of your investigation results dated [Date of Bureau Response] regarding my dispute, sent on [Date of Original Dispute Letter] under USPS Certified Mail Tracking Number [Original Tracking Number], of the account associated with [Creditor Name], Account Number [Account Number].

Your response indicated that the disputed item was "verified as accurate." I respectfully but firmly reject that conclusion. The information remains factually incorrect, and I do not believe a reasonable reinvestigation under FCRA §611(a) was actually conducted.

Pursuant to my right under FCRA §611(a)(7), 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(7), I now demand that your agency provide a description of the procedure used to determine the accuracy and completeness of the disputed information. The statute is unambiguous: this description must be provided within fifteen (15) days of your receipt of this request.

I.  SPECIFIC INFORMATION YOU MUST PROVIDE

The description required by 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(7) must address my specific dispute, not your agency's dispute process in general. To satisfy the statute, your response must identify, with respect to the account referenced above:

1.  The business name, address, and telephone number of every furnisher of information contacted in connection with this dispute, as expressly required by 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(7).

2.  The full name, title, and direct contact information of every individual at your agency who participated in the reinvestigation of this specific dispute.

3.  The specific procedure that was used to verify the accuracy and completeness of the disputed information for THIS account — not a generic description of how your agency processes disputes generally.

4.  Whether the verification was performed solely through the automated Electronic-Online Solution for Complete and Accurate Reporting (e-OSCAR) system and the corresponding Automated Consumer Dispute Verification (ACDV) form. If so, identify the specific ACDV code transmitted to the furnisher, the specific code returned by the furnisher, and the date and time of each transmission.

5.  Copies of any documents the furnisher provided to your agency in support of the verification, including but not limited to: the original signed contract or application, the account ledger or transaction history, payment records, and any other underlying documentation reviewed.

6.  The full text of any narrative, comment, or supplemental information transmitted by the furnisher in connection with the verification.

II.  GENERIC DESCRIPTIONS WILL NOT SUFFICE

A boilerplate description of your agency's general dispute-handling process will not satisfy 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(7). The statute, by its express terms, requires a description of the procedure used to determine the accuracy of the specific disputed information. A response that does not identify the specific furnisher contact, the specific verification procedure, and the specific documents reviewed for THIS account is, on its face, noncompliant.

Federal courts have repeatedly held that a consumer reporting agency's blind reliance on an automated furnisher response — particularly where the consumer has provided specific documentary evidence of inaccuracy — does not constitute the "reasonable reinvestigation" required by 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(1)(A). If your agency cannot identify any verification procedure beyond the transmission of an ACDV code, that fact is itself evidence that no reasonable reinvestigation occurred.

III.  IF YOU CANNOT PRODUCE THE PROCEDURE, YOU MUST DELETE

If your agency cannot provide the specific procedural description required by 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(7), or if the description reveals that no genuine verification procedure was followed, you must DELETE the disputed item from my credit file. An item that cannot be verified through a reasonable procedure cannot lawfully remain on my consumer report under FCRA §611(a)(5)(A)(i).

IV.  CONSEQUENCES OF NONCOMPLIANCE

Failure to provide the required description within the statutory fifteen-day window — or provision of a generic, non-responsive form letter that fails to address the specific items above — will be treated as a willful violation of FCRA §611(a)(7) and a continuation of the underlying §611(a) violation. Both expose your agency to civil liability under 15 U.S.C. § 1681n, including statutory damages of up to $1,000.00 per violation, actual damages, punitive damages where supported, and reasonable attorney's fees and costs. I will additionally file complaints with the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and the Office of the Attorney General for the State of [Your State of Residence].

I am preserving this letter, the certified-mail tracking record, your agency's prior verification response, and any response your agency provides to this letter. I expect your agency to preserve the same.

I look forward to your timely and substantive compliance with 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(7).

Sincerely,

[Your Signature]
[Your Printed Name]

Enclosures:
- Copy of original dispute letter dated [Date of Original Dispute Letter] with USPS Certified Mail receipt
- Copy of bureau's verification response dated [Date of Bureau Response]
- Copy of credit report with the disputed item clearly marked
- Copy of state-issued photo identification
